english: Stronger than 'abhängig von.' You are directed toward what you need. Auf + Akkusativ. 'Angewiesen' means 'pointed toward.' Your need points at the source of help.
Adverb. 'Da' (there/then) + 'mals' (time).\nMore precise than 'früher.'
'Da' (there/then) + 'mals' (time). Points to a specific time in the past. More precise than 'früher' (earlier/in the past). 'Damals in Berlin' sets a clear temporal frame.
Adverb. From Old High German 'einest.'\nLiterary and elevated. More poetic than 'damals.'
From Old High German 'einest' (once). Literary and elevated. More poetic than damals. Used in fairy tales: 'Es war einst...' The word itself sounds like a memory fading.
Adverb. 'Heute' (today) + 'zu' (to) + 'Tage' (days).\nThe contrast partner to 'damals' and 'einst.'
'Heute' (today) + 'zu' (to) + 'Tage' (days). Three words fused into one adverb. The contrast partner to damals and einst. Damals vs. heutzutage frames every life story.
The preposition determines the case. Auf + Akkusativ, von + Dativ, mit + Dativ, für + Akkusativ.
Angst vor + Dativ. You stand before what frightens you.\n'Die Angst vor der Zukunft' (the fear of the future).
Angst vor + Dativ. Vor means 'before/in front of.' You stand before what frightens you. English borrowed 'angst' directly from German. Freud made Angst a worldwide word.
Einfluss auf + Akkusativ. Your influence flows onto something.\n'Der Einfluss auf die Entscheidung' (the influence on the decision).
Ein (in) + Fluss (flow). Influence literally means 'inflow.' Your Einfluss flows into a decision. Auf + Akkusativ because the influence is directed onto something.
Hoffnung auf + Akkusativ. You direct your hope onto something.\n'Die Hoffnung auf eine bessere Zukunft.'
Hoffnung auf + Akkusativ. 'Die Hoffnung stirbt zuletzt' (Hope dies last) is a German proverb. You direct your hope onto something ahead of you. Auf points forward.
Bedarf an + Dativ. What you require, what you need at hand.\n'Der Bedarf an Fachkräften' (the need for skilled workers).
Bedarf an + Dativ. An here means 'at/of.' Your need exists at the thing required. 'Bedarf anmelden' (to register a need). A formal word common in business and policy.
Freude über + Akkusativ. Your joy flows over the cause.\n'Die Freude über die Rückkehr' (the joy about the return).
Freude über + Akkusativ. Über (about/over). 'Freude schöner Götterfunken' is the opening of Schiller's Ode to Joy, set by Beethoven. The EU anthem. Freude is powerful.
Stolz auf + Akkusativ. Same preposition as the adjective stolz auf.\n'Der Stolz auf die eigene Leistung.'
Stolz auf + Akkusativ. The noun version matches the adjective: stolz auf. Same preposition, same case. 'Nationalstolz' (national pride) is complicated in Germany since 1945.
Erinnerung an + Akkusativ. Your memory reaches toward the past.\n'Die Erinnerung an die Kindheit.'
Erinnerung an + Akkusativ. An (at/directed at). Your memory reaches at the past. 'Erinnerungsstück' (memento). Related to 'erinnern' (to remember), which brings something back into your inner self.
Wunsch nach + Dativ. You wish after something.\n'Der Wunsch nach Geborgenheit' (the wish for security).
Wunsch nach + Dativ. Nach (after/toward). You wish after something you do not yet have. 'Wunschdenken' (wishful thinking). 'Sich etwas wünschen' (to wish for something).
Sehnsucht nach + Dativ. The deepest German emotion with its\nfixed preposition. You yearn after what is distant.
Sehnsucht nach + Dativ. The most German of emotions with its fixed preposition. Romantic poets built entire philosophies around Sehnsucht nach dem Unerreichbaren (longing for the unreachable).
Vertrauen in + Akkusativ. You place your trust into something.\n'Das Vertrauen in die Zukunft.'
Vertrauen in + Akkusativ. In (into). You place your trust into something. 'Vertrauensverlust' (loss of trust). Trust flows into its object. Building Vertrauen takes time; losing it takes a moment.
Dank für + Akkusativ. Same pattern as dankbar für.\n'Der Dank für die Unterstützung.'
Dank für + Akkusativ. 'Dank' as noun means 'thanks/gratitude.' 'Dankeschreiben' (thank-you letter). Same preposition as dankbar für. 'Zum Dank für' (as thanks for).
Interesse an + Dativ. Your interest is located at the subject.\n'Das Interesse an fremden Kulturen.'
Interesse an + Dativ. An (at). Your interest exists at the subject. 'Interessenkonflikt' (conflict of interest). The noun matches the adjective: interessiert an + Dativ.
Irgend (some) + wann (when). Not a specific time, but eventually.\n'Irgendwann komme ich zurück.'
The irgend- prefix adds vagueness to question words. Irgendwann (some-when), irgendwo (somewhere), irgendwer (someone). The series creates beautiful imprecision.
From Kunft (coming, as in Zukunft). What is coming toward us.\n'Künftige Generationen' (future generations).
From Kunft (coming). The adjectival form of Zukunft. 'Künftig' can also be an adverb: 'Künftig esse ich weniger Fleisch' (From now on I will eat less meat). The future is always arriving.
Superlative of lang used as adverb. Something completed far in\nthe past. 'Das habe ich längst gewusst.'
Superlative of 'lang' used as adverb. 'Längst vergessen' (long since forgotten). 'Längst überfällig' (long overdue). It implies the action was completed well before now.
Seit (since) + dem (then). Marks the starting point of ongoing change.\nBoth conjunction and adverb.
Seit (since) + dem (then). As conjunction: 'Seitdem ich hier lebe...' As adverb: 'Seitdem hat sich alles verändert.' Marks when change began and that it continues.
Von (from) + je (ever) + her (from there). Since the beginning of\ntime. Elevated and literary.
Von (from) + je (ever) + her (from there). 'Von jeher' reaches all the way back to the beginning. More literary than 'schon immer.' Fairy-tale language that elevates everyday observations.
Zeit + Lebens (of life). A single adverb covering an entire lifespan.\n'Zeitlebens suchte er eine Heimat.'
Zeit (time) + Lebens (genitive of Leben). One word for an entire lifetime. 'Zeitlebens' collapses a whole biography into a single adverb. Powerful in its compression.
From Old High German 'seula.' German Romanticism made the\nSeele central to philosophy and poetry.
From Old High German 'seula.' Hegel, Schelling, Novalis: all wrote about the Seele. 'Die Seele baumeln lassen' (to let the soul dangle) means to relax deeply. A uniquely German image.
Ruhe (calm) + Pol (pole). The still point in a spinning life.\n'Berlin ist mein Ruhepol.'
Ruhe (calm) + Pol (pole). Like the North Pole on a compass: a fixed point that everything else spins around. 'Er ist der Ruhepol der Familie' (He is the family's anchor).
Ob (over/above) + Dach (roof). The most basic level of Heimat.\n'Obdachlose' lack this roof.
Ob (over) + Dach (roof). 'Obdachlos' (homeless) means literally 'without an over-roof.' The most basic form of Heimat: having a Dach. Without Obdach, Geborgenheit is impossible.
Zu (to) + Flucht (flight). Where you flee to. Not running away\nbut running toward safety.
Zu (to) + Flucht (flight). 'Zuflucht suchen' (to seek refuge). 'Zufluchtsort' (place of refuge). You are not fleeing from something but fleeing to safety. The zu makes all the difference.
Mutter (mother) + Land. More personal than Vaterland.\nThe land that mothered you.
Mutter (mother) + Land. The nurturing homeland. More emotional than Vaterland. Also used metaphorically: 'Das Mutterland des Jazz' (the motherland of jazz). The land that gave birth to something.
Vater (father) + Land. Historically loaded in German.\nModern Germans prefer 'Heimat' or simply 'Deutschland.'
Vater (father) + Land. 'Fürs Vaterland' (for the fatherland) was the slogan of two World Wars. After 1945, the word carries heavy historical baggage. Modern Germans use it carefully, if at all.
From Mut (mood/spirit). When your inner nature is at peace,\nthe world feels gemütlich.
From Mut (mood/spirit). 'Gemütlich' (cozy) comes from Gemüt. When your inner nature is peaceful, everything around you feels gemütlich. 'Gemütlichkeit' is Germany's coziest export.
Sicherheit (security) + s + Gefühl (feeling). With Fugen-s.\nWhat Geborgenheit provides.
Sicherheit (security) + s + Gefühl (feeling). The Fugen-s links the compounds. At three words deep, this is classic German: Sicherheit-s-gefühl. What Geborgenheit provides in concrete form.
Ver- + Wurzel (root) + -t. Past participle as adjective.\nYour roots have grown deep.

Ver- + Wurzel (root) + -t. 'Tief verwurzelt in der Gemeinschaft' (deeply rooted in the community). The adjective captures a state of belonging that took time to develop. Trees need years. So do people.
Zu (to) + Frieden (peace). At peace with what you have.\nNot needing more. 'Zufrieden mit dem Leben.'
Zu (to) + Frieden (peace). 'Zufriedenheit' (contentment). Being content means being at peace with what you have. Not needing more. The most German form of happiness: quiet satisfaction.
Ort (place) + s + bezogen (related). Memories tied to specific\nlocations. With Fugen-s.

Ort (place) + s + bezogen (related). With Fugen-s. 'Ortsbezogene Erinnerungen' (place-related memories). Memories tied to specific locations. Also used in tech: 'ortsbezogene Dienste' (location-based services).
Ruhe (calm) + liebend (loving). A compound present participle.\nSomeone who loves calm.
Ruhe (calm) + liebend (loving). A compound present participle used as adjective. 'Ein ruheliebender Mensch' loves Ruhe. The opposite of 'gesellig' (sociable). Not antisocial, just peace-seeking.
Ver- + Wurzel + -n. Reflexive: sich verwurzeln.\nThe process of growing roots in a new place.
Ver- + Wurzel (root) + -n. Not separable. 'Sich in einer Gemeinschaft verwurzeln' (to take root in a community). The process version of verwurzelt. Growing roots is active work.
Be- + Heimat + -en. Non-separable. The city performs the act\nof being a Heim.
Be- + Heimat + -en. Non-separable. 'Berlin beheimatet 3,7 Millionen Menschen.' The be- prefix makes Heimat into a verb. A city does the act of being a home.
Ein (in) + leben (to live). Reflexive: sich einleben.\nSeparable: 'Ich habe mich eingelebt.'
Ein (in) + leben (to live). Separable. 'Sich einleben' means living yourself into a new place. The ein- prefix suggests entering. You enter a new life by living into it.
Two meanings: valuing and guessing. 'Ich schätze das sehr'\n(I appreciate that). 'Ich schätze, es dauert eine Stunde.'
From 'Schatz' (treasure). To treasure something means to appreciate it. The second meaning (to estimate) comes from estimating the value of treasure. Two meanings from one root.
Ort (place) + s + Kenntnis (knowledge). With Fugen-s.\nKnowing where the bakery is, which shortcut to take.
Ort (place) + s + Kenntnis (knowledge). With Fugen-s. Before GPS, London taxi drivers had to pass 'The Knowledge.' German Ortskenntnis is the equivalent: knowing your city by heart.
Zugehörigkeit (belonging) + s + Gefühl (feeling). With Fugen-s.\nAt 24 letters, a classic German compound.
Zugehörigkeit (belonging) + s + Gefühl (feeling). At 24 letters, a classic German compound. The feeling of belonging somewhere. What Verumius finally found after two years of searching.
Ver- + Sprechen (speaking). When you speak forth, you bind\nyourself. Also: 'sich versprechen' = to misspeak.
Ver- + Sprechen (speaking). 'Ein Versprechen halten' (to keep a promise). 'Ein Versprechen brechen' (to break a promise). Also: 'sich versprechen' means to misspeak. Two meanings, one verb.
Verwandt (related) + -schaft. The collective noun for everyone\nyou are related to. Often invoked at Weihnachten.

Verwandt (related) + -schaft (collective suffix). 'Weitläufige Verwandtschaft' (distant relatives). The -schaft suffix groups individuals into a collective. Your Verwandtschaft is everyone who shares your blood.
Zuverlässig (reliable) + -keit. What Germans value above\nalmost everything else.
Zuverlässig (reliable) + -keit. 'Deutsche Zuverlässigkeit' is both a stereotype and a real cultural value. 'Sich auf jemanden verlassen' (to rely on someone). Zuverlässigkeit makes trust possible.
As modal particle (not 'yes'): adds emphasis or surprise.\n'Das ist ja toll!' (That is wow great!)
Ja as particle does not mean 'yes.' It signals the speaker's reaction to something unexpected or notable. 'Du bist ja schon da!' (You're already here!). One of the first particles learners master.
Contradicts, reassures, or insists. 'Komm doch mit!'\n(Come along, won't you!). The most versatile particle.
The most versatile German particle. Can mean 'after all,' 'but,' 'yes it is,' 'please do.' 'Doch' can be a one-word sentence answering a negative question with 'yes.' Untranslatable perfection.
From einmal (once). Makes requests casual and friendly.\n'Schau mal!' (Look!). Without mal, requests sound blunt.
From 'einmal' (once). 'Schau mal!' (Look!). 'Hör mal!' (Listen!). Makes requests casual and friendly. Without mal, German requests sound military. With mal, they sound human.
Signals resignation or acceptance. 'Das ist eben so.'\n(That's just the way it is.) No point arguing.

Eben as particle: 'Das ist eben so' (That's just the way it is). Calm resignation. Also means 'just now': 'Ich war eben dort' (I was just there). And 'flat/level': 'eine ebene Fläche' (a flat surface). Three meanings.
Southern German/Austrian equivalent of eben. More colloquial.\n'Das ist halt so.' (That's just how it is.)
Southern German/Austrian equivalent of eben. More colloquial, warmer. Lukas would say halt, Dr. Yilmaz would say eben. Regional particles reveal where you learned your German.
Expresses probability or concession. 'Er wird wohl schon\nzu Hause sein.' (He is probably already home.)
Wohl as particle hedges without committing. 'Er wird wohl kommen' (He will probably come). Also: 'Wohl oder übel' (willy-nilly, like it or not). Wohl softens claims into probabilities.
Introduces the real truth behind appearances.\n'Eigentlich wollte ich bleiben.' (Actually, I wanted to stay.)

'Eigentlich' introduces the real truth. 'Was willst du eigentlich?' (What do you actually want?). In questions it adds genuine curiosity. In statements it corrects: 'Eigentlich mag ich Berlin' (Actually, I like Berlin).
As particle (not conjunction): makes questions warmer.\n'Was machst du denn?' sounds friendlier than 'Was machst du?'
Denn in questions adds interest and warmth. 'Was machst du denn?' vs 'Was machst du?' The first sounds curious, the second sounds like an interrogation. Denn humanizes questions.
Reassures or concedes. 'Das wird schon klappen.'\n(It will work out, don't worry.)
Schon as particle calms fears. 'Das wird schon' (It'll be fine). Also concedes: 'Du hast schon recht' (You're right, I suppose). Reassuring and slightly resigned at the same time.
Adds urgency or desperation. 'Mach das bloß nicht!'\n(Whatever you do, don't do that!)

Bloß as particle intensifies. 'Wo ist bloß mein Schlüssel?' (Where on earth is my key?). 'Mach das bloß nicht!' (Don't you dare!). Adds desperation or urgency. Also means 'merely': 'bloß ein Kind' (merely a child).
Permission given calmly. 'Du kannst ruhig bleiben.'\n(You can go ahead and stay, it's fine.)
Ruhig (calm/quiet) as particle: permission given calmly. 'Nimm ruhig noch ein Stück Kuchen' (Go ahead and take another piece of cake). The speaker is calm about it. Take it, no problem.
Two uses: questioning tone (really? don't tell me!) and\napproximation. 'Hast du etwa Angst?'
Etwa as particle challenges: 'Hast du etwa Angst?' (Don't tell me you're afraid!). As adverb: 'etwa 50 Leute' (approximately 50 people). The same word questions and estimates.
Nun (now) + mal (just). Double particle for double resignation.\nStronger than eben or halt.
Nun (now) + mal (just). Double particle for double resignation. 'Das ist nun mal so' is stronger than 'Das ist eben so.' There is truly nothing to be done, and everyone knows it.
Durch (through) + aus (out). Emphatic agreement.\n'Das ist durchaus möglich.' (That is absolutely possible.)
Durch (through) + aus (out). All the way through. 'Durchaus nicht' (absolutely not). 'Durchaus möglich' (absolutely possible). The word goes through-and-out, leaving no doubt.
Ohne (without) + hin (there). No matter what: the outcome was\nalready decided. A calm dismissal of alternatives.
Ohne (without) + hin (there). 'Ich wollte ohnehin gehen' (I was going to leave anyway). The action happens regardless of what you say. Calmer and more formal than sowieso.
So + wie + so. More colloquial than ohnehin. Both mean the\nsame: the action happens regardless.
So + wie + so. The most casual way to say 'anyway.' 'Ich wollte sowieso...' (I was going to anyway). More colloquial than ohnehin. Both dismiss alternatives, but sowieso does it with a shrug.
Zu (to) + rück (back). Both adverb and prefix.\nzurückkommen, zurückgehen, zurückdenken.
Zu (to) + rück (back). The direction of return. As prefix it creates dozens of verbs: zurückkommen (come back), zurückgehen (go back), zurückdenken (think back), zurücklassen (leave behind).
Abschied (farewell) + nehmen (to take). You take the farewell.\nActive: you choose to let go.
Abschied (farewell) + nehmen (to take). 'Abschied nehmen von' + Dativ. You actively take the farewell. Not passive: you choose to let go. Every German departure deserves a proper Abschied.
Fort (away) + ziehen (to pull/move). Separable: 'Er zog fort.'\nPulling yourself away from where you are.

Fort (away) + ziehen (to pull/move). Separable. Perfect with sein: 'Er ist fortgezogen.' Fort implies distance: not just leaving but going far. 'Fortziehen und nie zurückkommen' (to move away and never come back).
Ge- + hören (to hear/belong). Not separable.\n'Ich gehöre hierher.' (I belong here.)

Ge- + hören (to hear). Two meanings: 'Ich gehöre hierher' (I belong here) and 'Das gehört mir' (That belongs to me). One of the deepest verbs in the Heimat vocabulary. Belonging is hearing your place.
Nach (after) + trauern (to mourn). Separable. Mourning after\nsomething that has moved away.
Nach (after) + trauern (to mourn). Separable: 'Er trauert der alten Zeit nach.' The nach implies the object has already moved away. You mourn after what is gone. Healthier than clinging.
Zurück (back) + kehren (to turn). Not separable.\nUses sein: 'Er ist zurückgekehrt.'
Zurück (back) + kehren (to turn). Separable despite appearances: 'Er kehrt zurück.' Perfect with sein: 'Er ist zurückgekehrt.' Synonym of rückkehren. Turning back to where you started.
Zurück + lassen (to let/leave). Separable: 'Er ließ alles zurück.'\nWhat you choose not to bring with you.

Zurück + lassen (to let/leave). Separable. Strong verb: zurücklassen/ließ zurück/zurückgelassen. What you choose not to bring with you. 'Alles zurücklassen' (to leave everything behind) is the hardest kind of Aufbruch.
Zusammen (together) + halten (to hold). Separable.\nThe verb behind Zusammenhalt.
Zusammen (together) + halten (to hold). Separable. Strong verb: zusammenhalten/hielt zusammen/zusammengehalten. The verb behind Zusammenhalt. What keeps a group unified when things get difficult.
Ent- (un-/de-) + Wurzel (root) + -n. The opposite of verwurzeln.\nTearing roots out. What migration does before new roots grow.

Ent- (un-/de-) + Wurzel (root) + -n. The opposite of verwurzeln. Tearing roots out. 'Entwurzelte Menschen' (uprooted people). Migration uproots before new roots can grow. The ent- prefix reverses the process.
To nurture, maintain, cultivate. 'Freundschaften pflegen'\n(to cultivate friendships). The nurturing verb.
'Freundschaften pflegen' (to cultivate friendships). 'Traditionen pflegen' (to maintain traditions). 'Kranke pflegen' (to care for the sick). The nurturing verb. What Hildi did for Verumius's plants.
To coin, to stamp, to shape. 'Die Erfahrung hat mich geprägt.'\nWhat your Heimat does to you.
'Prägen' originally means to stamp coins. What your Heimat does to you: it stamps itself into your identity. 'Geprägt von der Erfahrung' (shaped by the experience). The stamp is permanent.
